message:
chore(beads): close bd-1eqi io_uring capability contract task

Marks the io_uring capability contract task as completed — the ASX_HAS_IO_URING
config macro and browser boundary assertion were landed in the previous commit.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:

 {"id":"bd-16r","title":"Add JSON schema validation to CI pipeline","description":"8 JSON schemas exist in schemas/ but no CI step validates generated artifacts against them. Create tools/ci/validate_schemas.sh and wire into make check / ci.yml.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-02T04:37:17.794469672Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-02T04:40:00.767801508Z","closed_at":"2026-03-02T04:40:00.767780318Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ci","quality"]}
 {"id":"bd-1bq","title":"Add NULL guards to budget.c public API functions (S2 audit)","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-03-02T05:27:56.697416590Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-02T05:29:41.335508977Z","closed_at":"2026-03-02T05:29:41.335484912Z","close_reason":"Added NULL guards to all 7 public budget.c functions: meet, consume_poll, consume_cost, is_exhausted, is_past_deadline, polls. NULL budget returns safe defaults (identity for meet, 0/exhausted for queries). All tests pass.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-1cg","title":"Audit and update traceability index status rows","description":"docs/PLAN_EXECUTION_TRACEABILITY_INDEX.md has multiple rows stuck at in_progress that may now be complete. Audit TRC-INV-001, TRC-GSM-001/002/003, TRC-BUILD-001.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-02T04:43:14.619372674Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-02T04:44:46.717317596Z","closed_at":"2026-03-02T04:44:46.717295685Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["docs"]}
-{"id":"bd-1eqi","title":"Add explicit io_uring capability contract and browser/profile assertions","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-25T04:50:07.609305522Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-25T04:50:56.194487973Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"bd-1eqi","title":"Add explicit io_uring capability contract and browser/profile assertions","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-25T04:50:07.609305522Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-25T04:52:13.482256482Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T04:52:13.481251921Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-1eqo","title":"Substantive core parity roadmap for /dp/asupersync","description":"We have working kernel slices in C, but we are still far from replicating what a Rust user would reasonably call the core functionality of `/dp/asupersync`. This epic is the replacement for the original markdown plan: it should be detailed enough that a future contributor can recover scope, rationale, ordering, validation strategy, and user-facing intent directly from the bead graph.

Core parity here means substantially covering the Rust surfaces that make asupersync feel like asupersync for real users: foundational value/type contracts (`id`, `outcome`, `cancel`, `budget`, `policy`, `pressure`, `resource`, `rref`, `task_context`, `symbol`, `typed_symbol`, `wasm_abi`), explicit `Cx` capability flow, `Scope`/structured concurrency, runtime state/configuration, deterministic scheduler behavior, timer/deadline-driven control flow, channel and sync semantics, orchestration combinators, seeded lab runtime and replay oracles, bytes/codec/io/net/app scaffolding, observability and diagnostics, authority/security hardening, stream and service composition, and the native host surfaces needed to exercise the runtime outside toy harnesses.","design":"Roadmap structure:
1. Freeze a source-backed scope definition so future work is measured against `/dp/asupersync`, not memory.
2. Build an explicit verification/provenance plan before implementation so parity claims are evidence-backed rather than retrospective.
3. Establish foundational type/value parity early so every higher-level API is built on the same IDs, outcomes, budgets, task-context, symbol, and typed-payload contracts Rust users rely on.
4. Grow outward from semantic core to usable public surface: types/value contracts -> `Cx`/`Scope` -> runtime object/scheduler/reactor -> time/cancel -> channels/sync -> combinators/streams/plan -> lab/replay/oracles -> bytes/io/net/app -> observability/security/native-host -> actor/supervision follow-on.
5. Treat unit tests, invariant tests, conformance/lab scenarios, and logged end-to-end scripts as first-class deliverables, not cleanup work.
6. Keep intentionally deferred areas visible in the graph so they cannot disappear from planning just because they are not in the first implementation wave.","acceptance_criteria":"A. The backlog contains explicit epics and tasks for every major subsystem required to reach substantive Rust-core parity and the near-core host surfaces needed to exercise it.
B. Each issue is self-contained: background, intended approach, acceptance criteria, verification expectations, and rationale are stored in bead fields.
C. Dependency edges are strong enough that `br ready` surfaces the intended implementation order and `bv --robot-*` analysis highlights the true unblockers.
D. The graph records where unit, invariant, conformance/lab, and logged end-to-end evidence must be produced so verification cannot be silently skipped.
E. A future contributor can reconstruct the port strategy from the bead graph alone without reopening the original markdown planning documents.","notes":"Source surfaces used to define this epic: `/dp/asupersync/README.md`, `/dp/asupersync/src/lib.rs`, `/dp/asupersync/Cargo.toml`, the Rust source tree under `/dp/asupersync/src`, and the local canonical docs `docs/EXISTING_ASUPERSYNC_STRUCTURE.md`, `docs/FEATURE_PARITY.md`, and `PLAN_TO_PORT_ASUPERSYNC_TO_ANSI_C.md`.

This revised roadmap intentionally promotes observability/diagnostics, security/audit/authority hardening, stream/plan/service composition, native host surfaces, the exported `types` family, canonical example/smoke-script workflows, and Browser Edition / WASM contract surfaces from implied follow-on work into explicit beads. That makes the plan more truthful to the Rust crate’s exported core story and better aligned with what an actual user would need to judge the C port as usable rather than merely semantically interesting.

Still downstream and intentionally not in the immediate core wave: the largest transport/application stacks such as full HTTP/2-3, gRPC, TLS-heavy integration, database clients, distributed/messaging/web frameworks, and other expansion areas beyond the core plus Browser Edition contract path. Those should eventually receive their own backlog branches, but this roadmap now captures the minimum self-contained program needed before that expansion.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:40.382303984Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-13T04:11:06.707042844Z","closed_at":"2026-03-13T04:11:06.703127957Z","close_reason":"All roadmap children completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["core-parity","roadmap","rust-reference"]}
 {"id":"bd-1eqo.1","title":"Freeze Rust-core scope and gap inventory against /dp/asupersync","description":"Freeze the actual scope of Rust-core parity before implementation advances further. This planning epic exists to replace vague memory and optimism with a source-backed model of what users of `/dp/asupersync` actually experience as the core product.","design":"This planning epic should be decomposed because one giant inventory task becomes a false bottleneck. We need separate outputs for:
1. exported/module surface inventory,
2. user-facing workflow and persona inventory,
3. semantic gap/risk/dependency ranking,
4. profile/resource-class capability matrix and user-visible limits.

Those outputs together define what "core parity" means in a way that is implementable, testable, and meaningful to actual users.","acceptance_criteria":"A. The roadmap decomposes scope-freezing into granular planning tasks rather than a single oversized blocker.
B. We have explicit source-backed views of exported surfaces, user workflows, semantic risk ordering, and profile/resource-class expectations.
C. Downstream planning and implementation beads can depend on the specific inventory slice they need instead of a vague umbrella task.
D. The resulting plan is more parallelizable and less likely to hide planning mistakes behind a single mega-bead.","notes":"Reasoning: the original `bd-1eqo.1` was directionally correct but still too coarse. In graph terms it became an articulation point for too much of the roadmap. Splitting it keeps the plan self-documenting while reducing false bottlenecks.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:40.515952264Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-13T03:27:53.769721494Z","closed_at":"2026-03-13T03:27:53.765277520Z","close_reason":"All 4 children completed: Rust inventory, workflow inventory, gap ranking, capability matrix.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["core-parity","inventory","planning"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1eqo.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1eqo","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:40.515952264Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1eqo.1.1","title":"Inventory exported Rust modules, public contracts, and implied semantic surfaces","description":"Map the exported Rust surface and adjacent public contracts into a precise parity inventory so we know exactly which source modules, guides, examples, and policy documents define the target.","design":"Inventory `/dp/asupersync/src/lib.rs`, module exports, README guarantees, canonical docs/guides that materially define user-visible behavior, example and smoke-script surfaces, and feature/profile policy documents that change the exposed contract. The output must group source modules and contract docs by parity track and distinguish direct public surface from adjacent implied behavior the runtime must support.","acceptance_criteria":"A. The roadmap contains a source-backed inventory of exported Rust modules and the public contracts they imply.
B. Each major roadmap track can point to the modules, guides, and guarantees it is responsible for matching.
C. The inventory distinguishes public API surface from merely internal implementation detail.
D. Browser/WASM guides, doctor/report contracts, and canonical example surfaces are included when they materially define the user-visible product.
E. Notes are detailed enough that a future contributor does not need to reopen `lib.rs`, README, and contract docs just to remember what the target surface includes.","notes":"This is the module-and-contract map. It answers "what exists?" and "which Rust sources make that existence authoritative?"","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-12T17:25:52.332195511Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-12T18:08:35.698388198Z","closed_at":"2026-03-12T18:08:35.697980555Z","close_reason":"Completed: added docs/RUST_EXPORTED_SURFACE_INVENTORY.md mapping the upstream exported modules, root re-exports, and adjacent public contracts to bd-1eqo roadmap tracks","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["exported-surface","inventory","planning"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1eqo.1.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1eqo.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-12T17:25:52.332195511Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
